title: Strategic Optionality Inventory

content.statement: Good strategy preserves or creates valuable future choices.
content.question: What's one decision on your plate that would quietly close off a future option? Can you preserve that option cheaply?
content.equation: SOI = Σ(Option Value × Reachable Prob × Time Window × Relevance)
sop: List your major upcoming decisions and, for each, note what future options they open or close.
Flag any decision that closes a door you're not fully sure you want closed yet.
Look for a cheaper version of that decision that preserves the option, a pilot instead of a full commitment, a month-to-month instead of a multi-year lock-in.
Rate Option Value, Reachable Probability, Time Window, and Relevance 1-10 for your top 3 open options (see calculation, page 6)
Review your option inventory quarterly, some options expire if you don't use them.
content.explainer: The best moves buy you options. The worst ones quietly kill them. Track the option inventory.
questions: What's one decision on your plate that would quietly close off a future option? Can you preserve that option cheaply?
Which current commitment is limiting choices you might want in 12 months?
If you had to list 3 doors you've kept open on purpose, could you name them right now?

definitions.def: The value of having future choices.

content.equation_example: Rate Option Value, Reachable Probability, Time Window, and Relevance 1-10 for each option you're tracking, then sum across your top options. Option 1: 8×7×6×5 = 1,680. Option 2: 6×5×4×7 = 840. SOI = 1,680 + 840 = 2,520. Higher SOI means you're holding valuable, real, timely choices open; lower means your option inventory is thin or theoretical.
content.closing_statement: The best moves quietly buy you more choices.

caption:
Principle Nº 36: Strategic Optionality Inventory

Good strategy preserves or creates valuable future choices. The best moves buy you options.

Ask yourself: What's one decision on your plate that would quietly close off a future option? Can you preserve that option cheaply?
